title: I am safe even when the number is small
subtitle: A short morning affirmation for the days the bank-balance number is the first thing on the mind.
excerpt: A short morning affirmation for the days when the bank-balance number is the first thing on the mind. One sentence said three times in the morning, three in the evening, hand on the centre of the chest. The work is the repetition over weeks, not the single morning.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release fear of running out". The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, daily repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).

content.text: A short morning affirmation for the days the bank-balance number is the first thing on the mind. One sentence said three times in the morning, three in the evening, hand on the centre of the chest. The work is the repetition over weeks, not the single morning.

Some weeks the bank-balance number is small. Pay day is still days away, the bills have already gone out, the buffer is gone. The body learns to read "small number" as "unsafe"; for many women, that link was laid down in childhood, in the household script, in the family lineage. The affirmation is the counter-sentence. It does not change the number; it changes what the number means.

attrs.quote: I am safe even when the number is small.

content.text: Three times in the morning. Hand flat on the centre of the chest. Say the sentence slow, out loud if the room allows it, in your mind if it does not. Each word lands before the next.

Three times again in the evening, the same way. Or at the moment the number gets checked in the day: opening the banking app, the supermarket till, the petrol pump. Three repetitions at the moment of looking.
Stay with this one sentence through one full tight week. Switching to a different affirmation each day stops any of them from landing.

If "safe" feels too far away on a particular day, drop to a smaller true version. "I am here in this moment." "I am breathing." Find the version the body accepts. Returning to the bigger sentence the next morning is the work. For a longer release-and-allow reset,

Adapted from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program by Rebecca J Page, 2025. The affirmation pairs with the day's tapping script on "what if it runs out". The shape is plain affirmation craft: present tense, positive phrasing, daily repetition. The form goes back to the New Thought movement and was widely shared by writers like Louise Hay in You Can Heal Your Life, 1984.
